0: 2nd International Conference on Sustainable Energy and Resource Use in Food Chains, ICSEF 2018, 17-19 October 2018, Paphos, Cyprus
1: <header>Environmental impacts of poultry litter gasification for power generation </header>
Harish K. Jeswani, Andrew Whiting, Alastair Martinb , Adisa Azapagica

"Centre for Sustainable Energy use in Food chains (CSEF), Sustainable Industrial Systems, School of Chemical Engineering and Analytical Science, The University of Manchester, M13 9PL, United Kingdom Department of Engineering, Engineering Building, Lancaster University, Bailrigg, Lancaster LAI 4YW, United Kingdom
2: The poultry industry generates a large quantity of litter, which consists of a mixture of manure, waste bedding and feathers, and can be used for recovery of energy. Recent studies suggest that gasification is a promising technology for an effective conversion of poultry litter to energy. This paper aims to assess the environmental impacts of generating electricity from the gasification of poultry litter in a small-scale integrated gasification combined cycle (IGCC) plant. Gasification of poultry litter has been simulated with ASPEN Plus software and life cycle assessment has been used to determine the carbon footprint and primary energy demand of the system. The following life cycle stages have been considered: collection and storage of poultry litter, its gasification, syngas cleaning, production of electricity and co-production of ash as a fertiliser. The carbon footprint and primary energy demand of generating 1 kWh of energy by poultry litter gasification plant are estimated at 42 g CO2 eq./kWh and 0.14 MJ/kWh, respectively. Compared to electricity from fossil fuels, this system has 91%-96% smaller carbon footprint and 98%-99% lower primary energy demand. The energy payback period is estimated at 1.5 years.

Keywords: Integrated gasification; poultry litter; life cycle assessment; carbon footprint; primary energy demand;

4: The poultry sector is growing globally to meet the increasing demand for poultry meat and egg products, driven by population growth, urbanisation and rise in income. Global per-capita consumption of poultry meat increased from 3.1 kg to 15 kg between 1964 and 2013, while global per-capita consumption of eggs grew from 4.7 kg to 9.2 kg [1]. In 2016, the worldwide poultry sector reared around 66 billion chickens for meat [1].
5: FB|fluidised bed HHV|higher heating value IGCC|integrated gasification combined cycle LCA|life cycle assessment PED|primary energy demand

The poultry sector also generates a large quantity of litter, which consists of a mixture of manure, waste bedding, waste food and feathers. The amount of litter depends on the frequency of the removal of litter, which varies from country to country. The reported quantity of poultry litter generated per bird over the production cycle in the literature ranges from 1.5 to 5.7/kg of litter/bird [2]. Traditionally, poultry litter has been utilised as a fertiliser and soil conditioner as it is rich in plant nutrients nitrogen, phosphorus and potassium [3]. However, due to growing environmental and social concerns associated with the land spreading of poultry litter, alternative treatment options are becoming attractive, including energy recovery through thermal treatment, such as combustion [4], pyrolysis [5], and gasification [6].

Poultry litter has a physical appearance and consistency similar to a mixture of wood chips and saw dust. Besides, due to a relatively high concentration of volatiles and low moisture content, poultry litter is a suitable feedstock for energy recovery via thermal and thermochemical processes [2]. Currently, it is combusted to produce electricity at several commercial plants in Europe and North America [7]. Life cycle assessment (LCA) studies suggest that combustion of poultry litter has a lower environmental impact compared to land application [8, 9].

Although thermal treatment processes can be effective in energy conversion, thermochemical treatment such as gasification provides some advantages over direct combustion, including better energy recovery [2]. Gasification also offers greater flexibility as it produces a product, syngas, which can either be used in gas engines or boilers for heat and electricity production or can be upgraded into methanol and chemical feedstocks via Fischer-Tropsch process [10].

A number of studies have assessed the potential of gasification of poultry litter for different types of gasifiers [6, 11, 12]. According to these studies, fluidised bed (FB) gasifier has higher efficiency compared to other types of gasifiers. FB gasifiers are also flexible regarding the material in the feed and ease in maintaining temperature control below the melting point of ash [13]. Although recent studies suggest that gasification is a promising technology to make an effective conversion of poultry litter into energy, its environmental impacts are at present unclear. Thus, this paper aims to assess the environmental impacts of generating electricity from the gasification of poultry litter. The results are compared with the impacts of electricity from fossil-fuel alternatives. A sensitivity analysis is performed to explore the effect of changing certain parameters.
6: FB|fluidised bed HHV|higher heating value IGCC|integrated gasification combined cycle LCA|life cycle assessment PED|primary energy demand

Besides, due to a relatively high concentration of volatiles and low moisture content, poultry litter is a suitable feedstock for energy recovery via thermal and thermochemical processes [2]. Currently, it is combusted to produce electricity at several commercial plants in Europe and North America [7]. 8: The goal of this LCA study is to quantify the carbon footprint and primary energy demand associated with generating electricity in a small-scale integrated gasification combined cycle (IGCC) plant using poultry litter as a feedstock. LCA is conducted following the ISO 14040/44 guidelines [14, 15]. The functional unit is defined as 'generation of 1 kWh of electricity'. The system boundary, as shown in Fig. 1, includes: collection and storage of poultry litter, its gasification, syngas cleaning, combustion of syngas, electricity generation and co-production of ash as a fertiliser. Construction and decommissioning of the plant are also considered.

The study considers a fluidised bed gasification IGCC plant for generating electricity. The installation capacity of IGCC plant is 250 kW which is suited to a medium size broiler farm producing 75,000 birds annually. The plant is situated close to a broiler farm to avoid the need for transportation. The litter is collected at the farm and transferred into a storage tank using a digger. The storage tank has a capacity to hold the litter of one rearing cycle and would supply the feedstock to gasifier for six weeks. Emissions of nitrous oxides (N20), a potent greenhouse gas, during the storage have been calculated as per recommended guidelines [16]. Assuming an average of a four-week storage period, it is estimated that 80 g of N2O are emitted per tonne of stored litter.

The litter is gasified in a FB gasifier to produce syngas and ash. The gasification process has been modelled through simulation in ASPEN Plus®, based on the composition of poultry litter shown in Table 1 [6]. After passing through the gas cleaning system, the syngas is combusted in a combined cycle power system to produce electricity. The residual heat from the exhaust gases is recuperated to preheat air for gasification and combustion in order to increase the efficiency. The IGCC plant has gross efficiency of 43% [17]. About 15% of the electricity produced is used as a parasitic load to operate pumps, motor and control equipment. The remaining electricity is available to be used on the farm or exported to the grid.

The ash is removed using a cyclone and a filter and can be used as a fertiliser in agriculture, replacing synthetic potassium and phosphorus fertilisers. The system has been credited for the displacement of fertilisers in accordance with the system expansion approach. The ash composition, including K2O and P2O5 content has been obtained from the literature [18]. Since the availability to the plants of potassium and phosphorus in the ash is typically 50% lower than in synthetic fertilisers due to immobilisation [19], the credits have been applied accordingly. Table 2 provides a summary of input and output data for the system. The background LCA data for infrastructure, machinery, transport and other relevant processes have been sourced from Ecoinvent v3.3 [20].
Fig. 1. System boundary for gasification of the poultry litter to recover energy

2: The carbon footprint and primary energy demand of generating 1 kWh of electricity from gasification of poultry litter are estimated at 42 g CO2 eq./kWh and 0.14 MJ/kWh, respectively (Fig. 2a). Without the credits for avoided impacts from synthetic fertilisers, these values would be 59 g CO2 eq./kWh and 0.5 MJ/kWh. Operation of the plant contributes 50% to the total carbon footprint and 85% to the energy demand (before the credits). This is largely due to the use of zeolite in the gasifier and CH4 and N2O emissions from syngas combustion. The other main contributor to the carbon footprint is N2O emission from the litter storage, which accounts for 41% of the total impact (before the credits). The credits received for the production of P2O5 and K2O fertilisers and recycling of metals at the end of the plant's lifespan reduce the total carbon footprint by 17 g CO2 eq./kWh and primary energy demand by 0.35 MJ/kWh. The contributions of plant construction and transport are small (Fig. 2a).

Compared to electricity from fossil fuels (natural gas, coal, and oil), this system would result in a saving the carbon footprint of 91%-96% and in the primary energy demand of 98%-99% (Fig. 2b). The poultry litter gasification system assessed in the study is estimated to have an energy payback time of 1.5 years.
Fig. 2. (a) Carbon footprint and primary energy demand (PED) for generation of 1 kWh of electricity from poultry litter gasification; (b) Comparison with fossil fuel alternatives
3: The carbon footprint and primary energy demand of generating 1 kWh of electricity from gasification of poultry litter are estimated at 42 g CO2 eq./kWh and 0.14 MJ/kWh, respectively (Fig. 2a). Fig. 2. (a) Carbon footprint and primary energy demand (PED) for generation of 1 kWh of electricity from poultry litter gasification; (b) Comparison with fossil fuel alternatives
4: The sensitivity analysis considers the effect of some key parameters on the impacts. These are related to the storage time for litter, disposal of ash and allocation. The findings are discussed below for each parameter in turn.

Storage time for litter: In the base case, it has been assumed that litter is stored for four weeks, and for the sensitivity analysis, two options are considered: two weeks and six weeks. Results in Fig. 3 show that the carbon footprint would decrease by 30% if the storage time is reduced to two weeks; for a six-week storage period, the carbon footprint would increase by 30%.

Landfilling of ash: The ash is assumed to be used as a fertiliser in the base case. An alternative would be to landfill it. In that case, the carbon footprint and PED would increase by 50% and 2.5 times, respectively. This is mainly due to the absence of the credits for the displacement of the synthetic fertilisers applied in the base case.

Economic allocation for ash: In the base case, the impacts between electricity and fertilisers in ash have been allocated through system expansion via the credits for the fertilisers in ach. If instead economic allocation is used based on the wholesale prices of electricity [22] and fertilisers [23], the carbon footprint would increase by 20% and PED by two times relative to the base case (Fig. 3).
Fig. 3. Influence of different parameters on the carbon footprint and primary energy demand of generating 1 kWh of electricity via poultry litter gasification
5: The sensitivity analysis considers the effect of some key parameters on the impacts. These are related to the storage time for litter, disposal of ash and allocation. The findings are discussed below for each parameter in turn.

Storage time for litter: In the base case, it has been assumed that litter is stored for four weeks, and for the sensitivity analysis, two options are considered: two weeks and six weeks. Results in Fig. 3 show that the carbon footprint would decrease by 30% if the storage time is reduced to two weeks; for a six-week storage period, the carbon footprint would increase by 30%.

Landfilling of ash: The ash is assumed to be used as a fertiliser in the base case. An alternative would be to landfill it. In that case, the carbon footprint and PED would increase by 50% and 2.5 times, respectively. This is mainly due to the absence of the credits for the displacement of the synthetic fertilisers applied in the base case.

Economic allocation for ash: In the base case, the impacts between electricity and fertilisers in ash have been allocated through system expansion via the credits for the fertilisers in ach. If instead economic allocation is used based on the wholesale prices of electricity [22] and fertilisers [23], the carbon footprint would increase by 20% and PED by two times relative to the base case (Fig. 3).
Fig. 3. Influence of different parameters on the carbon footprint and primary energy demand of generating 1 kWh of electricity via poultry litter gasification
6: This study has assessed the environmental impacts of generating electricity from poultry litter in a small-scale IGCC plant. The findings indicate that, compared to the fossil-fuel alternatives, the savings in the carbon footprint and primary energy demand exceed 90%. Reducing storage time of litter would also result in further savings in the carbon footprint due to the reduction in N2O emissions. This finding, coupled with the offensive odours from decomposition of poultry litter during storage, suggests that the storage time should be kept as short as possible. The sensitivity analysis shows that the environmental impacts are influenced by the ash disposal and allocation methods. However, even at the least favourable conditions related to these two parameters, the carbon footprint and primary energy are still significantly lower than for the fossil-fuel alternatives. The LCA results show that using poultry litter to generate electricity in a small-scale integrated gasification combined cycle plant is an environmentally viable option. However, the successful application of this technology will depend on the future reduction in the installation costs.
7: This study was conducted as part of the UK Centre for Sustainable Energy Use in Food Chains (CSEF), funded by the UK Research Councils (EP/K011820/1). This funding is gratefully acknowledged.
